perturbation.original_text: 1.16 Commercially Reasonable Efforts The term "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" shall mean, with respect to the performance of an obligation under this Agreement, such quality and level of effort as is required to carry out such obligation in a sustained manner, consistent with the efforts Roche or FMI, as applicable, devotes to a similar obligation in connection with an internally developed product or service that is at the same stage of development or commercialization, as applicable, in a similar market, with similar market potential, at a similar stage of product life, taking into account the existence of other competitive products or services in the market place or under development, the proprietary position of the product or service, the regulatory structure involved, the anticipated profitability of the product or service and other relevant factors. It is understood that such quality and level if effort may change from time to time based upon changing scientific, business and marketing and return on investment considerations.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.16 Commercially Reasonable Efforts The term "Commercially Reasonable Efforts" shall mean, with respect to the performance of an obligation under this Agreement, such quality and level of effort as is required to carry out such obligation in a sustained manner, consistent with the efforts Roche or FMI, as applicable, devotes to a similar obligation in connection with an internally developed product or service, irrespective of its stage of development or commercialization. The existence of other competitive products or services in the market place or under development, the proprietary position of the product or service, the regulatory structure involved, the anticipated profitability of the product or service and other relevant factors are explicitly excluded. It is understood that such quality and level if effort may change from time to time based upon changing scientific, business and marketing and return on investment considerations.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' is modified to exclude factors normally considered, thereby weakening the obligation and contradicting common legal interpretations.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-306(1) implies good faith and reasonable effort in exclusive dealing contracts. By excluding consideration of market factors, the modified definition allows for arbitrary reduction of effort, contradicting the implied obligation of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines 'Commercially Reasonable Efforts' by explicitly excluding relevant market factors. UCC § 2-306(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, implies good faith in exclusive dealing contracts. Excluding the consideration of market factors allows for arbitrary reduction of effort, which can be argued as acting in bad faith, thereby creating a contradiction. The modification weakens the obligation to an extent that it might not align with reasonable commercial standards typically considered during contract enforcements.

perturbation.original_text: 1.17 Confidential Information The term "Confidential Information" shall mean any and all information, data or know-how (including Know-How), whether technical or non- technical, oral or written, that is disclosed by one Party or its Affiliates ("Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates ("Receiving Party"). Confidential Information shall not include any information, data or know-how that: (i) was generally available to the public at the time of disclosure, or information that becomes available to the public after disclosureby the Disclosing Party other than through fault (whether by action or inaction) of the Receiving Party or its Affiliates, (ii) can be evidenced by written records to have been already known to the Receiving Party or its Affiliates prior to its receipt fromthe Disclosing Party, (iii) is obtained by the Receiving Party at any time lawfully from a Third Party under circumstances permitting its use or disclosure, (iv) is developed independently by the Receiving Party or its Affiliates as evidenced by written records other than throughknowledge of Confidential Information, or (v) is approved in writing by the Disclosing Party for release by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.17 Confidential Information The term "Confidential Information" shall mean any and all information, data or know-how (including Know-How), whether technical or non- technical, oral or written, that is disclosed by one Party or its Affiliates ("Disclosing Party") to the other Party or its Affiliates ("Receiving Party"). Confidential Information shall not include any information, data or know-how that: (i) was generally available to the public at the time of disclosure, or information that becomes available to the public after disclosure by the Disclosing Party through fault (whether by action or inaction) of the Receiving Party or its Affiliates, (ii) can be evidenced by written records to have been already known to the Receiving Party or its Affiliates prior to its receipt from the Disclosing Party, (iii) is obtained by the Receiving Party at any time lawfully from a Third Party, (iv) is developed independently by the Receiving Party or its Affiliates, or (v) is approved in writing by the Disclosing Party for release by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is modified by removing the requirement for written records in exceptions (ii) and (iv), and removing the condition 'under circumstances permitting its use or disclosure' in (iii), making it easier to argue information is not confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), 18 U.S. Code § 1839
perturbation.law_explanation: DTSA protects trade secrets, which overlaps with Confidential Information. By broadening exceptions to what constitutes confidential information, this modification weakens the protection of potential trade secrets, increasing the risk of misappropriation which is a violation of this act.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the exceptions to the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the 'written records' requirement in exceptions (ii) and (iv), and the phrase 'under circumstances permitting its use or disclosure' in exception (iii). This weakening of protection for potential trade secrets could increase the risk of misappropriation, contradicting the intent of trade secret protection laws, such as the DTSA, as explained by the LLM. Snippet 1, 18 USC 1839, establishes definitions related to trade secret protection, and by making it easier for information to fall outside the definition of 'Confidential Information', the modified text increases the likelihood of trade secrets being unprotected and potentially misappropriated, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 1.36 FTE The term "FTE" shall mean a full-time equivalent person-year, based upon a total of no less than one thousand eight hundred (1,800) working hours per year, undertaken in connection with the conduct of research in a Work Stream. In no circumstance can the work of any given person exceed one (1) FTE.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.36 FTE The term "FTE" shall mean a full-time equivalent person-year, based upon a total of no less than one thousand eight hundred (1,800) working hours per year, undertaken in connection with the conduct of research in a Work Stream. The work of any given person can exceed one (1) FTE at the discretion of the JMC.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of FTE is modified to allow for work exceeding 1 FTE, contradicting the basic concept of full-time equivalence and potentially impacting labor law compliance if not managed properly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) - 29 U.S. Code § 207
perturbation.law_explanation: While FLSA does not directly define FTE, it regulates overtime pay based on hours worked. Allowing a person to exceed 1 FTE implies work beyond standard full-time hours, potentially triggering overtime requirements that could be circumvented by misinterpreting the FTE definition, thus contradicting the spirit and potential application of FLSA regarding overtime.

The Child Labor Rules Advisor answers questions about the FLSA’s youth employment provisions, including at what age young people can work and the jobs they can perform. The Section 14(c) Advisor helps users understand the special minimum wage requirements for workers with disabilities. perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit of labor laws, particularly concerning overtime, as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text defines FTE as not exceeding one (1) FTE. The changed text allows exceeding 1 FTE at the discretion of the JMC. This contradicts the standard understanding of 'full-time equivalent' and can lead to potential circumvention of overtime regulations. Snippet 1 mentions regulations regarding overtime pay due, implying that the FLSA addresses overtime even if it doesn't explicitly define FTE. Allowing employees to exceed 1 FTE could be a way to avoid paying required overtime, resulting in non-compliance.
